text: We are presenting robotic-assisted right adrenalectomy with IVC thrombectomy. We have no conflict of interest to disclose. Patient, a fifty-eight-year-old male with diabetes mellitus, incidentally detected of right adrenal mass with IVC thrombus, Hb of twelve and creat of point ninety-three. MRI suggested of six into four point four into six point four centimeter right adrenal mass with level two IVC thrombus with query IVC volume region, probability of adrenocortical carcinoma.

The MRI coronal and axial sections display the right adrenal tumor and the IVC thrombus. The Da Vinci SI robotic system is used for the procedure. The patient is in a supine position as the surgical team mobilizes the hepatic lobe and loops the supra-hepatic IVC and porta hepatis.

The patient is in a supine position with visible port placements for robotic assistance. The falciform ligament is divided, and the liver is dissected from its anterior attachments. The left lobe of the liver is mobilized, followed by the division of the gastro-hepatic ligament. The right lobe of the liver is also mobilized.

The surgeon begins paracaval dissection, focusing on the tissue anterior to the suprahepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC). Multiple short hepatic veins are clipped and cut. An infant feeding tube is passed from the right side of the liver and retrieved from the left side, allowing the suprahepatic portion of the IVC to be looped and controlled. The porta hepatis is looped using a cut Foley catheter.

The surgeon dissects tissue anterior to the infrahepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC), exposing the anterior wall of the IVC. The patient is positioned in the right flank position, and ports are placed for the robotic-assisted right adrenalectomy with IVC thrombectomy.

The surgeon dissects the ascending colon, carrying the dissection medially and laterally to the inferior vena cava (IVC) in the suprarenal portion. The suprarenal IVC is looped, and dissection proceeds between the right adrenal tumor and the upper pole of the kidney, as well as between the lateral wall of the IVC and the adrenal tumor. The adrenal gland and tumor are separated from all their attachments.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to locate the IVC thrombus, clips and cuts additional hepatic veins, and loops the infrahepatic portion of the IVC above the thrombus. Clamps are applied, and an incision is made at the junction of the right adrenal vein and IVC. The IVC thrombectomy is performed, and the thrombus along with the right adrenal tumor is set aside.
The surgeon flushes the inferior vena cava (IVC) and closes the cavotomy using a 5-0 Gotic suture, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.

The surgeon releases the IVC clamps after flushing the upper and lower sutures with heparin and tying a knot in the middle. There is no bleeding from the IVC, and hemostasis is complete. The specimen is placed in a bag, and control of the porta hepatis and supra-hepatic IVC is released. The cut section of the specimen is shown, indicating no postoperative complications.
